text.title: Survey Question Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Good and bad survey question examples with explanations of why each antipattern fails.

example.code: Question: "How often do you use the search feature?" Options: - Never - Less than once a week - 1-3 times per week - Daily - Multiple times per day
example.note: Single concept, unambiguous options with no gaps or overlaps, no assumed direction.

example.code: Question: "How satisfied are you with [product]?" Options: - Very dissatisfied - Dissatisfied - Neutral - Satisfied - Very satisfied
example.note: Standard CSAT wording; symmetric scale from negative to positive; all five points labelled.

antipattern.description: Double-barreled: "How satisfied are you with the speed and accuracy of search results?"
antipattern.reason: Two dimensions in one question — a user who finds search fast but inaccurate cannot give a meaningful answer. Split into two separate questions.

antipattern.description: Leading: "How much do you love our new feature?"
antipattern.reason: Presupposes positive sentiment; drives respondents toward positive answers regardless of actual experience. Neutral wording: "How would you rate the new feature?"

antipattern.description: Vague: "How often do you use it?"
antipattern.reason: "It" is undefined; "often" is undefined. Every respondent interprets the question differently, producing incomparable data.

antipattern.description: Double negative: "Is there anything you don't dislike about the onboarding?"
antipattern.reason: Double negatives require cognitive parsing effort that introduces noise and errors in responses. Rewrite as a direct positive or negative question.

list.item: Too long — target under 5 minutes; surveys over 10 minutes see 40%+ dropout.
Too many open-ended questions — each open-ended adds analysis cost; limit to 1-2 per survey.
No pilot test — unclear questions only become visible when real respondents encounter them.
Wrong audience — sample that does not represent actual users produces misleading findings.
No analysis plan — define how you will analyze results before fielding, not after; post-hoc fishing inflates false-positive rates.
